plainMeaning: This is the confession for the eleventh vow — the day-long monk-like retreat (pauṣadha) — listing its four kinds and the careless slips a layperson asks forgiveness for after keeping it.
recitation: 11. igyāramuṁ pauṣadhavrata, cihuṁ bhede jāṇavuṁ. caturvidha āhāra posahe — caturvidha āhāra-taṇo parihāra. sarīra-sakkāra posahe — sarvathā snānādika śarīra-śuśrūṣā-taṇo parihāra. baṁbhacera posahe — sarvathā audārika, vaikriya maithuna-taṇo parihāra. avvāvāra posahe — sarvathā sāvadya vyāpāra-taṇo parihāra. e igyāramā pauṣadhavrata-taṇā pāñca atiyāra śodhuṁ. appaḍilehiya duppaḍilehiya sijjā-saṁthāre, appamajjiya duppamajjiya sijjā-saṁthāre, appaḍilehiya duppaḍilehiya uccāra pāsavaṇa bhūmi, appamajjiya duppamajjiya uccāra pāsavaṇa bhūmi. posahovavāsassa sammaṁ aṇupālaṇāyā. poṣaha kīdhe śayyā, upāśraya, saṁthāro, saṁthārā-taṇī bhūmikā ane mala-mūtra-taṇāṁ thaṁḍila, thaṁḍila-taṇī bhūmikā, divasa chate paḍilehyāṁ, dṛṣṭe joyāṁ na hoya. ane vastrāñcale, daṇḍāsane karī paḍilehyāṁ pramājaryāṁ na hoya, athavā virūi-pare paḍilehyāṁ pramājaryāṁ hoya. posaha-taṇo upavāsa samyak sācavyo na hoya, caturvidha āhāramāṁ eke āhāra vāñchyo hoya. ane snānādika śarīra satkāra, abrahma-sevā, vāṇijyādika gṛha-vyāpāra vāñchyā hoya. pāchalāṁ smaryāṁ hoya, anāgata prārthyāṁ hoya, śarīra chāṁṭyāṁ hoya, āloṭā dīdhā hoya. posaha thake pāraṇānī maṁtraṇā kīdhī hoya. divase lāṁbe page saṁthāryuṁ hoya. śleṣmādi ughāḍo mūkyo hoya. upāśrayathī nīkaḷatāṁ āvassahī, pesatāṁ nissihī kahī na hoya. porisi bhaṇyā pākhe rātre saṁthāryuṁ hoya. ujjei saṁghaṭṭa huo hoya. aneruṁ e igyāramā pauṣadhavrata viṣe.

wordByWord.notes: Marks the eleventh confession-block, the pauṣadha-vrata.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ordinal of igyāra 'eleven' (< Skt. ekādaśa)
Skt. pauṣadha 'spiritual-nourishing observance' + vrata 'vow'; pauṣadha < poṣa 'nourishment'
wordByWord.notes: First of the four śikṣāvratas reviewed here as the eleventh of the twelve.
Book's reference asterisk; not recited.
Gerundive: 'should be understood as four-fold.'
First sub-type: āhāra-pauṣadha.
The four kinds of food: aśana, pāna, khādima, svādima.
Refrain term: each pauṣadha-type is a 'parihāra' (renunciation).
Second sub-type: śarīra-satkāra-pauṣadha.
Total renunciation, stronger than the partial daily restraint.
Third sub-type: brahmacarya-pauṣadha.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. audārika 'pertaining to the gross (udāra) body'
wordByWord.notes: Jain body-type term: physical, earthly bodies.
The protean bodies of gods/hell-beings; renounced along with the gross.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit avvāvāra < Skt. avyāpāra 'absence of occupation'
wordByWord.notes: Fourth sub-type: avyāpāra-pauṣadha.
Standard Jain term for karmically harmful action.
Closes the fourfold definition.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. aticāra; book's spelling with ય

wordByWord.notes: The recurring purification-formula opening each block.
Pratilekhanā = the ritual visual checking for life-forms.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sijjā < Skt. śayyā 'bed' + saṁthāra < saṁstāra 'spread bedding'
wordByWord.notes: saṁstāra corresponds to Tattvārtha 7.30's saṁstaropakrama atichāra.
Pramārjanā = gentle sweeping with the soft broom to remove minute beings.
The disposal-ground; corresponds to the utsarga atichāra of Tattvārtha 7.30.
Genitive Prakrit form embedded in the canonical formula.
Covers the anādara + smṛtyanupasthāna atichāras of Tattvārtha 7.30 — not keeping the fast carefully or attentively.
Begins the Gujarati gloss/expansion of the five faults.

wordByWord.gloss: having undertaken / when one has done

wordByWord.notes: Where the pauṣadha is kept.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit thaṁḍila < Skt. sthaṇḍila 'a cleared/bare patch of ground'
wordByWord.notes: The lifeless ground designated for voiding.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. chatuṁ 'existing' (< Skt. sat), locative absolute
wordByWord.notes: 'While daylight still existed' — i.e. no excuse of darkness.
Confession idiom: 'if it were so / should it have happened.'

wordByWord.gloss: with the corner of the cloth
wordByWord.notes: Tool of pratilekhanā.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. daṇḍa 'staff' + āsana; the soft-bristled cleaning broom
wordByWord.notes: Used for pramārjanā (sweeping away minute life).

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. virūiṁ 'wrongly' (< Skt. virūpa 'mis-shaped') + pare 'in the way'
wordByWord.notes: I.e. inspected/swept but badly.
The fifth atichāra: not maintaining the fast properly.
Mental breach: even longing for food during the fast is a fault.
Craving the renounced śarīra-satkāra.
Craving the renounced brahmacarya.
Craving the renounced avyāpāra.
Brooding on past enjoyments breaks the retreat's focus.
Hankering after future pleasures.
Wetting the body — a covert form of the renounced bathing, harming water-bodied beings.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. āloṭavuṁ 'to roll on the ground'
wordByWord.notes: Tossing/rolling for bodily ease.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pāraṇā 'completion of a fast' + genitive -nī

wordByWord.notes: Plotting what to eat afterward — a mental lapse during the fast.
Lounging stretched-out by day, an unascetic posture.
Discharges left exposed (un-covered with ash/earth) breed and harm beings.
Said on leaving a sacred space to mark the necessity of the exit.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nissihī < naiṣedhikī 'the prohibitive (setting aside of worldly acts)'
wordByWord.notes: Said on entering, renouncing outside concerns.
Failure to say āvassahī/nissihī at the threshold.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit porisi < Skt. pauruṣī 'a watch of the night/day'
wordByWord.notes: The recitation done before lying down at the proper watch.
Lying down at night without first reciting the porisi.

wordByWord.etymology: uncertain; prob. Prakrit, related to Skt. ujjval 'to blaze, light' (lamp-light) — or 'unawares'
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; rendered tentatively. See notes.
An untoward touch/contact (e.g. with another, or with fire/light).
The closing catch-all formula of each block.
'…and whatever else (I transgressed) regarding the eleventh vow' — leading into the unspoken micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.
Closes the block.

literalTranslation: Eleventh, the pauṣadha-vow is to be known in four kinds. In the food-pauṣadha — the giving-up of fourfold food. In the body-care pauṣadha — the complete giving-up of bathing and other bodily grooming. In the celibacy-pauṣadha — the complete giving-up of intercourse, gross-bodied and transformation-bodied. In the non-activity pauṣadha — the complete giving-up of all sinful activity. These five transgressions of the eleventh pauṣadha-vow I scrutinise: un-inspected, ill-inspected bed and bedding; un-swept, ill-swept bed and bedding; un-inspected, ill-inspected excrement-and-urine ground; un-swept, ill-swept excrement-and-urine ground; (failure of) the proper maintenance of the pauṣadha-fast. When the pauṣadha was kept: the bed, the lodging, the straw-bedding, the bedding-spot, and the excretion-spot for dung and urine and its ground — while daylight was present — may not have been inspected nor examined by sight; and they may not have been inspected and swept with the cloth-corner and the broom-staff, or may have been inspected and swept improperly; the pauṣadha-fast may not have been properly kept; among the fourfold food some food may have been craved; and bathing and bodily adornment, indulgence in unchastity, trade and household business may have been craved; past things may have been dwelt upon, future things hankered after, the body sprinkled (with water), rollings indulged; while in the pauṣadha the fast-breaking meal may have been planned; by day one may have lain stretched-out-legged; phlegm and the like may have been left uncovered; on leaving the lodging 'āvassahī' and on entering 'nissihī' may not have been said; at night one may have lain down without reciting the porisi; an (untoward) contact may have occurred by lamplight. And whatever else (was transgressed) regarding this eleventh pauṣadha-vow.
idiomaticTranslation: The eleventh is the pauṣadha-vow, a day of living like a monk, to be observed in four forms: renouncing all four kinds of food; renouncing every bodily comfort — bathing, grooming and the rest; renouncing all sexual contact, whether with earthly or with celestial beings; and renouncing every sinful occupation. Now I examine its five transgressions. Did I lie on a bed or bedding I had not inspected, or inspected carelessly — or that I had not swept, or swept carelessly? Did I use a spot for relieving myself that was unchecked or ill-swept? Did I fail to keep the fast as it should be kept? When I held the pauṣadha, did I — though it was broad daylight — leave the bed, the hall, the straw-mat and its ground, and the place for dung and urine, un-inspected and unseen; or fail to inspect and sweep them with cloth-corner and broom; or do so clumsily? Did I keep the fast imperfectly, or even hanker after one of the four foods? Did I crave a bath or grooming, unchastity, or my trade and home affairs? Did I brood on past pleasures or long for future ones, splash water on my body, or roll about for ease? Did I, while still observing, plan my breaking-meal? Did I sprawl with legs outstretched by day, or leave phlegm and the like uncovered? Did I forget to say 'āvassahī' on going out and 'nissihī' on coming in? Did I lie down at night without reciting the porisi? Did some careless contact occur by the lamp? For all this, and whatever else I slipped in regarding this eleventh vow, may my misdeed come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the tenth vow narrowed the householder's spatial sphere, the eleventh asks him to step, for a fixed span, fully inside the monk's life. The verse opens with the standard four-fold analysis of pauṣadha — food, body-care, chastity and occupation — and crucially insists each be 'sarvathā', total, distinguishing the full (pratipūrṇa) pauṣadha from a partial one. The five canonical atichāras then arrive verbatim in their Prakrit liturgical form ('appaḍilehiya duppaḍilehiya…'), the same formula embedded in the Vandittu/Śrāvaka-pratikramaṇa: the paired un-inspected/ill-inspected and un-swept/ill-swept faults applied to two zones — where one sleeps (śayyā-saṁstāra) and where one relieves oneself (uccāra-pāsavaṇa-bhūmi) — capped by the fifth, failure to keep the fast 'sammaṁ', properly. What lifts this verse above a checklist is the long Gujarati amplification that follows: it re-reads those five abstract faults as the lived texture of a real retreat day. The danger is never the body's needs themselves but inattention to the minute lives disturbed by an un-swept mat or an exposed discharge; hence the twin instruments named — the vastrāñcala (cloth-corner) and daṇḍāsana (broom) — and the rebuke 'divasa chate', 'while it was still daylight', leaving no alibi. The list then turns inward, from the physical to the mental: not merely doing the renounced things but craving them — a bath, a meal, one's shop, a memory, a future plan. The two threshold-formulae āvassahī and nissihī and the night-time porisi show the retreat is also a discipline of speech and timing, not only of restraint. The block ends with the familiar 'aneruṁ…viṣe' catch-all, gathering every unnamed slip.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Pauṣadha is the householder's deliberate, time-bound rehearsal of muni-life, and the verse encodes a precise doctrinal point: a vow is broken not only by gross action but by careless attention. The five atichāras are, in substance, the five of Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.30 — apratyavekṣita-apramārjita applied to utsarga (voiding-ground), ādāna-saṁstāra (taking up and spreading bedding), plus anādara (disrespect) and smṛty-anupasthāna (lapse of mindfulness), here folded into 'posahovavāsassa sammaṁ aṇupālaṇāyā'. The governing principle is ahiṁsā operating through pratilekhanā (visual inspection) and pramārjanā (gentle sweeping): the sūkṣma jīvas of earth, water and the nigoda are protected precisely by looking and sweeping before lying, sitting or voiding. The amplification's stress on mere craving (vāñchyo, smaryāṁ, prārthyāṁ) reflects the Jain analysis of bhāva — that bondage tracks intention, so an unbroken outward fast spoiled by inward hankering is still flawed.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a reciter who keeps pauṣadha today, this verse is a concrete debrief of the day just past: did I actually inspect and sweep my mat and washing-area in daylight, or cut corners? Did I keep the fast cleanly, or quietly miss food, my phone, my work, my plans? Did I say āvassahī and nissihī at the threshold and recite the porisi before sleeping? Even for those who cannot keep a full pauṣadha, it trains a transferable habit — attentiveness to the small lives and small cravings that ordinary routine ignores.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.30 (five atichāras of pauṣadhopavāsa: apratyavekṣita-apramārjita-utsarga / -ādāna / -saṁstāra, anādara, smṛtyanupasthāna)
Upāsakadaśāṅga-sūtra (Ānanda's adoption of the twelve vows including pauṣadhopavāsa)
Vandittu / Śrāvaka-pratikramaṇa sūtra — the parallel Prakrit atichāra formula 'appaḍilehiya-duppaḍilehiya sejjā-saṁthāre…'

sources.consultedFor: Meaning and pratimā-context of pauṣadhopavāsa (the periodic monk-like fast).

sources.consultedFor: Sense of pauṣadha as the vow and the pauṣadha-house; darbha-bed and freedom from worldly activity.

notes: The five atichāras are given in their canonical Prakrit liturgical form and map onto Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.30; the long Gujarati passage following them is the book's amplified exposition, rendered closely. 'ઉજ્જેઇ સંઘટ્ટ હુઓ હોય' (ujjei saṁghaṭṭa huo hoya) is the least certain phrase: 'ujjei' is read tentatively as 'by lamplight' (an untoward contact with/near a lamp or another body) but could mean 'inadvertently/unawares'; the gloss is flagged uncertain pending the source-book footnote. 'virūi-pare' is taken as 'in a faulty/improper manner'. Per the book's orthography, 'atichāra' is preserved as અતિયાર and other Prakrit conjunct/spelling variants (sijjā-saṁthāre, posahovavāsassa) are kept verbatim, not normalised. Word-by-word coverage includes every repeated function word (posahe, parihāra, hoya, na, ane) and the closing daṇḍa.
